I apologize for taking so long to respond to your inquiry. 
At your request I have gone over Lee Rosenthal's letter 
concerning Mr. Nixon. 
In general, Mr. Rosenthal has correctly 
stated the law. 
An official is required to disquali 
himself 
from any decision if it is "reasonably foreseeable that the 
decision will have a material financial e fect, distinguish 
Ie 
from its effect on the public general "on a source of 
income of $250 or more in the previous year. 
Government 
Code Section 87103. 
In applying this standard, Mr. Rosenthdl 
correctly relied on 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703. 
Howeve, 
as Mr. Rosenthal's letter makes clear, it is not always e sy 
to determine, in a specific factual situation, whether dis-
qualification is necessary. 
As I understand it, no advice is presently being requested 
on this set of facts. 
We do not normally give advice concern n9 
decisions which have already been made. 
The remainder of Mr. Rosenthal's letter, and Ms. Cameron's 
letter, deal with the disclosure threshold for income to a 
business entity in which the filer has a 10 percent or gre te 
interest. 
Ms. Cameron's advice on this subject is correct. 
I hope this answers your questions, and I am sorry for 
the delay. 
If you have questions concerning the application 
of these principles to specific factual situations in the 
future, I will be happy to help you. 
BAM: 
h 
Ve 
truly yours, 
Barbara 
. Milman

Dear Ms. M.ilman: 
This letter is in follow-up to my conversation with you on 
January 27, 1981, askino if you would comment on the enclosed 
letter by Hr. Lee C. Rosenthall concerning the matter of poten-
tial conflict of interest of Mr. Jack Nixon, Chairman of the 
San Rafael Reoevelop~ent Aaency. 
The purpose of making this inquiry is to ascertain, essentially, 
if you agree with the opinion given to Mr. Nixon by Mr. Rosenthall. 
I would appreciate any comments you might have on this opinion. 
If you could respond prior to January 30th, it would be most 
appreciated. 
Thank you for your assistance in this matter. 
Very

STEVE:N H. GOLDFARB

Dear Mr. Beyer:

At the request of the Agency, I 
am writing with regard to 
the alleged conflict of interest of Agency member Jack Nixon 
in the matter of the negative declaration on the 3rd Street 
Improvement project. 
The facts as I understand them 
re as follows: 
is an insurance salesman. 
In his ins 
has received income of more than $250 
commissions on the sale of insurance 
McPhails has a retail busines 
that 
3rd and C Streets. 
The entrance 
it has adequate off-stree parking 
. Nixon 
s, Mr. Nixon 
o 
The decision about which the 
t ques ion 
arises involves issuance of a negativ 
under the 
California Environmental Ouality Ac 
with 
rd Street 
Improvement project. 
This project involves elimination of about 
50 parking spaces on 3rd Street and mak 
certain improvements in 
the traffic flow on that street. 
Of the 50 space 
eliminated. 
approximately 15 are within a block of McPhails. 
The project was 
proposed and approved by the Agency in 1978 allegedly without com-
pliance with The California Environmental 
1ity Act ("CEOA"). 
Owners of businesses on 3rd Street then brought suit against the 
Agency seeking to overturn the approval on grounds that CEQA was 
not complied in approving the project. 
At the request of the owners, 
the Superior Court issued a preliminary injunction 
ing imple-
mentation of the project. 
The Agency then 
rtified a negative 
declaration for the project, and the court thereafter dissolved the 
preliminary injunction. 
After the 
unction was dissolved, the Agency 
went forward with and completed the proj ct i 
1979. 
Trial of the owners' suit was held in 19 0, and the trial court 
RECEIVED 
.J 11 ;.,1 1 3 '1981 
flGENGV 


Robert Beyer 
January 12, 1980 
Page 2 
judge ordered the Agency to make a new 
terminat on 
to 
ther 
or not a negative declaration for the 
roject shou 
is ued. 
In its order the court specifically ordered the 
to consider 
the elimination of parking on 3rd Street in 1 
other projects 
that have been undertaken to provide off-
reet 
ing in the 
vicinity of the 3rd Street area. 
It is this new negative declaration 
determination that raises the conflict of interest question for 
Mr. Nixon. 
Under the Political Reform Act of 1974, an official cannot 
participate in a decision if it is "reasonably forseeable that the 
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable 
from its effect on the public generally," on a source of income 
from whom the official has received $250 in income in the previous 
year. 
Government Code Section 87103. 
Under the peculiar circumstances of this case, it is not reasonably 
forseeable that the decision with regard to the negative declaration 
will have a material financial effect upon Mr. Nixon's source of 
income, McPhails. 
To begin the decision before the Agency is not the 
substantive decision as to whether or not to do the project; but 
rather a procedural step, i.e. whether or not to prepare an environ-
mental impact report. 
That decision standing alone will have no 
financial effect on McPhails. 
In normal circumstances, the decision 
whether or not to prepare an environmental 
report is made as 
a preliminary step in the process of 
termin 
whether to undertake 
project. 
In those circumstanc 
preparation 
of the impact report conceivably 
au 
effect if it is reasonably forseeable 
substantive decision regarding the 
In this instance, however, the 
has already been made, and the proj 
t 
Therefore, it is highly unlike 
that 
will affect the SUbstantive decision 
Residents Ad Hoc Stadium Comm. v. Board 
project 
rried out. 
report 
ject. 
See 
, 89 Ca.App.3d 274 
Therefore, it cannot be said that it i 
sonab 
orseeable 
that the decision regarding the negative dcclara ion will affect the 
decision with regard to the project in such a manner so 
to have 
a material financial effect on McPhails. 
Even if we were dealing here with the 
proceed with the project, it would be di 
decision would have a rna 
rial financial 
p 
ect eliminated parking on 3rd S 
traffic flow on that street. 
I 
i 
ubs 
ve decision to 
cult to cone 
that the 
upon McPhails. 
The 
t altered the 
forseeable that 


Robert Beyer 
January 13, 1980 
Page 3 
removal of spaces will materially affect McPhails. 
The business is 
not oriented to 3rd Street but rather to C Street, and it has its 
own off-street parking. 
Thus, it is not forseeable that the elimin-
ation of the few spaces on 3rd Street in the iwmediate vicinity of 
McPhails would materially affect the volume of customers going to 
McPhails. 
Therefore, the income of the business would not be 
materially affected. 
It should be noted that regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission define "material financial effect" as an effect of $100,000 
or 1 
per year on gross income or $50,000 or 
2% per year on net 
income. 
2.Cal.Adm. Code Section 18703. 
It is hi 
ly unlikely that 
the elimination of parking on 3rd Street will have the magnitude of 
effect on McPhails to be considered material given the fact that 
McPhails has sales of five to ten million dollars or more a year. 
The other aspect of the project -
improvement of traffic flow 
in the downtown area of San Rafael -
may affect McPhails in that 
circulation in the area will be improved. 
However, all businesses 
in the downtown area will be affected in substantially the same manner 
by improved circulation. 
The segment of the population consisting 
of all downtown businesses is large enough to be considered the 
"public generally" for purposes of determining whether a material 
financial effect on a business is distinguishable from the effect 
on the public generally. 
In opinion Requested 
William L. Owen, 
Ci 
Attorney, City of Davis, 2 FPPC Ops.77 (1976). 
Thus, even if 
the traffic aspect of the decision affects McPhails materially, Mr. 
Nixon need not disqualify himself since the decision affects all 
downtown businesses in the same manner. 
With respect to Mr. Duggan's letter 
f 
5, 1980, to 
Hr. Nixon, a comment is appropriate. 
Contr(lry to Mr. Duggan's 
letter, the California Supreme Court, in 
Wood, 25 Cal.3d 
772 (1979), struck down Government Code 
(b) (2) as 
violative of equal protection and held 
the threshold for dis-
closure of income by brokers and a 
s 
Id be that provided 
in Government Code Section 87207 (b) (3) for other professions, i.e. f 
$10,000. 
Thus, contrary to the statements in Mr. Duggan's letter, 
Mr. Nixon's threshold for disclosure 0 
(l source of income from 
his insurance business on a statement of economic interest is 
$10,000 in a year, not $1,000 in a year. 
In his letter, Mr. Duggan also notes the construction of parking 
lots across from McPhails as a possible 
ource of 
can lict of 
interest. 
As I 
underst~and it, the con~;tru tion 
f thos 
lots was 
not part of the project for which the or 
ina1 nega ive declaration 
was issued. 
The effect of those lots obviou 
re 
s to the 
environmental impact of removal of 
rk 
in tha 
the 
of 
removal is mitigated by availability of alternative pa 
This 


Robert Beyer 
January 13, 1980 
Page 4 
issue is discussed in both the 1978 and 1980 initial studies for the 
negative declaration. 
However, I do not 
ee the 1980 negative 
declaration decision as being one prepared for the decision to build 
that off-street parking. 
Furthermore, even if the "project" for 
which the 1980 negative declaration is prepared is viewed as both 
t~e removal of parking from 3rd Street and the off-street parking, 
it is not forseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect on McPhails. 
Because the Agency has al 
purchased the land 
for and constructed the lots, it is highly unlike 
that 
decision 
re~arding the negative declaration will have any impact on a decision 
that has already been made and implemented at a large cost to the 
Agency.

sections.question_synthetic: Does an official need to disqualify themselves from decisions affecting a source of income, and what are the disclosure thresholds for clients/customers of a business entity in which the official has a 10% or greater interest?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: An official must disqualify themselves from decisions with a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on income sources of $250 or more. For business entities where the filer and spouse own more than 30%, disclosure is required only when a client/customer's gross receipts to the entity result in the filer's pro rata share of $10,000 or more. Disqualification may be required for sources between $250-$10,000, but disclosure of that source is not required until disqualification is determined.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document is OCR-scanned with multiple related letters. Primary FPPC response is from Barbara Milman dated March 3, 1981, with earlier response from Sarah T. Cameron dated January 15, 1981. OCR quality affects confidence; some text is garbled but main legal conclusions are identifiable.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does an official need to disqualify themselves from decisions affecting a source of income, and what are the disclosure thresholds for clients/customers of a business entity in which the official has a 10% or greater interest?

CONCLUSION: An official must disqualify themselves from decisions with a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on income sources of $250 or more. For business entities where the filer and spouse own more than 30%, disclosure is required only when a client/customer's gross receipts to the entity result in the filer's pro rata share of $10,000 or more. Disqualification may be required for sources between $250-$10,000, but disclosure of that source is not required until disqualification is determined.

Beyer City Manager City of San Rafael 1400 Fifth Avenue San Rafael, CA 94902 Dear Mr. Beyer: Administration 322·5660 Executive/legal 322-5901 March 3, 1981 Enforcement 322·6441 Slatements of Economic Interest 322·6444 I apologize for taking so long to respond to your inquiry. At your request I have gone over Lee Rosenthal's letter concerning Mr. Nixon. In general, Mr. Rosenthal has correctly stated the law. An official is required to disquali himself from any decision if it is "reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial e fect, distinguish Ie from its effect on the public general "on a source of income of $250 or more in the previous year. Government Code Section 87103. In applying this standard, Mr. Rosenthdl correctly relied on 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703. Howeve, as Mr. Rosenthal's letter makes clear, it is not always e sy to determine, in a specific factual situation, whether dis- qualification is necessary. As I understand it, no advice is presently being requested on this set of facts. We do not normally give advice concern n9 decisions which have already been made. The remainder of Mr. Rosenthal's letter, and Ms. Cameron's letter, deal with the disclosure threshold for income to a business entity in which the filer has a 10 percent or gre te interest. Ms. Cameron's advice on this subject is correct. I hope this answers your questions, and I am sorry for the delay. If you have questions concerning the application of these principles to specific factual situations in the future, I will be happy to help you. BAM: h Ve truly yours, Barbara . embedding.summary: FPPC responds to inquiries about conflict of interest standards for Jack Nixon, an Agency member, clarifying the disqualification threshold of $250 income and the $10,000 disclosure threshold for business entity clients/customers. The letter addresses the distinction between disqualification requirements and disclosure obligations under the Political Reform Act.
